name: fan_off_provenance_context_and_bookkeeping
description: A CA-issued WHF fan command is never misclassified as a manual override/cancel, while a genuinely external fan change is still correctly detected as manual (Issue #482)
notes: Occupant impact if this regresses: a fan transition CA itself caused (e.g. turning the
whole-house fan on to adopt nat-vent free cooling) gets misread as the user manually
touching the fan, latching a spurious grace period that can block CA from re-engaging
free cooling or HVAC for the configured grace window (manual_grace_seconds) -- the
occupant loses automated comfort/energy control for no real reason. Conversely, if the
new provenance check were made too aggressive, a REAL external fan action could be
silently swallowed and CA would keep acting as if it still owns the fan -- this scenario
checks both directions.
Covers Issue #482 Part 2 (event.context provenance) end-to-end through the real
coordinator: nat-vent adoption on sensor_open calls automation.py's _activate_fan(),
which now routes its fan.whf turn_on command through _call_fan_service_with_context()
-- a real HA Context is attached, FakeHass propagates it onto the resulting
state-changed event (tools/sim_harness/fake_hass.py), and coordinator._async_fan_entity_changed
reads event.context against automation_engine._fan_command_context_id, matching and
suppressing correctly (fan_ca_command_not_misclassified assertion).
The external_fan_state_change event then dispatches a state change with NO context at
all (fake_hass.states.async_set() called directly, not through a CA service call) --
modeling a genuine external actor (e.g. someone flips the physical switch). This must
still be classified as manual (on_fan_turned_off -> fan_cancel, trigger=fan_off) --
proving the Issue #482 context check is corroborating/additive, not a blanket suppression.
Part 1 (drift-reconciliation bookkeeping) is NOT independently exercisable end-to-end in
this harness -- confirmed via careful trace of the scheduler's task-queue interleaving
(FakeScheduler drains async_create_task coroutines strictly after the enclosing callback
returns): the misclassification only becomes reachable when a same-tick nat-vent
reactivation races the still-queued drift-reconciliation off-command, and _activate_fan()'s
own (pre-existing, sibling) unconditional finally-clear of _fan_command_pending stomps the
flag before the queued task's own event fires -- the single shared boolean is not
reentrant-safe against that specific interleaving, a deeper limitation shared by every
existing WHF command site, not unique to this fix, and out of scope for the literal
'match every other command site' fix this issue asked for. Part 1 is instead covered
precisely at the unit level (tests/test_fan_control.py::TestReconcileFanDriftBookkeeping),
which asserts the bookkeeping is stamped synchronously before the off-command task is
created and cleared after it completes -- see that file for the full analysis and the
revert-test proving it fails without the fix.
Three-exercise protocol (CLAUDE.md Sec 10):
(a) Scenario effectiveness: if automation.py's _call_fan_service_with_context stopped
attaching a Context (or coordinator.py stopped checking it), this scenario would
NOT independently catch a regression for the CA-command side, because
_fan_command_pending already independently suppresses that same transition (this is
the corroborating-signal design, not a replacement) -- so this scenario's real
regression-catching value is the external_fan_state_change assertion: if the new
context logic were ever changed to treat 'no context' as proof of CA-attribution
(an inversion bug), fan_external_change_classified would fail. Effectiveness for
the CA-side wiring itself (context flows through FakeHass, id comparison works, no
exceptions) is proven by this scenario passing at all -- a wiring break (e.g. a typo
in the attribute name) would surface as an exception in callback_errors.
(b) Docs-production alignment: docs/08-COMPUTATION-REFERENCE.md's fan-off classification
section should be updated to mention event.context as an additional corroborating
signal alongside the existing timing heuristic (Scribe follow-up).
(c) User outcome: verified above in the occupant-impact note.

events.note: Morning: indoor=72F, outdoor=60F -- outdoor well below indoor, favorable for nat-vent.
Mild day: hvac_mode=off, nat-vent eligible once a sensor opens.

Sensor opens. outdoor=60F < indoor=72F, indoor > comfort_heat=70F, outdoor <= threshold 78F -- nat-vent activates. _activate_fan() commands fan.whf on via _call_fan_service_with_context (Issue #482), carrying a real HA Context all the way through to the resulting state-changed event the real coordinator listener sees.

A genuinely external actor turns the fan off directly (no CA service call, no context) -- e.g. someone flips the physical switch. Must still be classified as manual (on_fan_turned_off -> fan_cancel, trigger=fan_off), proving the context check does not swallow real external actions.

assertions.reason: Sensor opened with favorable nat-vent conditions -- fan turns on, HVAC suppressed.

CA's own fan.whf turn_on command (issued via _call_fan_service_with_context, Issue #482) must not be misread by the real coordinator._async_fan_entity_changed listener as a manual fan-on -- no fan_manual_override/fan_cancel event may appear in this same cycle.

The external_fan_state_change event carries no CA context and was not preceded by a CA command -- the real coordinator listener must still classify it as manual (on_fan_turned_off -> fan_cancel, trigger=fan_off), proving Issue #482's context check is corroborating/additive only, not a blanket suppression that would leave CA blind to genuine user actions.

verdict.summary: CA-issued WHF commands carry event.context and are never misclassified as manual; genuinely external fan changes are still correctly detected (Issue #482)
verdict.observed_behavior: sensor_open -> natural_ventilation (CA-issued fan-on via _call_fan_service_with_context, no misclassification) -> external_fan_state_change -> fan_cancel (correctly classified as manual, trigger=fan_off)
verdict.expected_behavior: CA's own fan commands are attributed via event.context and never start a spurious grace period; real external fan actions are still detected as manual overrides
